flashcards.q: What is a cell?
flashcards.a: The basic structural and functional unit of life. The smallest unit capable of performing all life functions. All living organisms are made of one or more cells.

flashcards.q: State the Cell Theory (3 points).
flashcards.a: (1) All living things are made of cells. (2) The cell is the basic unit of life. (3) All cells arise from pre-existing cells (Virchow, 1855).

flashcards.q: What are the three basic features present in every cell?
flashcards.a: (1) Plasma membrane — outermost covering, selectively permeable. (2) Nucleus (or nucleoid in prokaryotes) — control centre. (3) Cytoplasm — fluid content with organelles.

flashcards.q: What is a selectively permeable membrane?
flashcards.a: A membrane that allows only certain substances to pass through it while blocking others. The plasma membrane is selectively permeable — allows gases, water (osmosis), and certain nutrients, but blocks other substances.

flashcards.q: Composition of the plasma membrane?
flashcards.a: Made of organic molecules — lipids and proteins. Can only be observed under an electron microscope.

flashcards.q: Define diffusion. Give an example in cells.
flashcards.a: Spontaneous movement of a substance from high concentration to low concentration — no energy needed. Example: CO₂ (waste) moves out of the cell; O₂ enters by diffusion.

flashcards.q: Define osmosis.
flashcards.a: The net movement of water molecules through a selectively permeable membrane from a region of high water concentration (low solute) to low water concentration (high solute).

flashcards.q: What happens to a cell in a hypotonic solution?
flashcards.a: Cell GAINS water by osmosis (external solution more dilute = higher water concentration) → cell SWELLS.

flashcards.q: What happens to a cell in an isotonic solution?
flashcards.a: No net movement of water → cell STAYS THE SAME SIZE.

flashcards.q: What happens to a cell in a hypertonic solution?
flashcards.a: Cell LOSES water by osmosis (external solution more concentrated = lower water concentration) → cell SHRINKS.

flashcards.q: What is endocytosis? Which organism uses it?
flashcards.a: Process by which the flexible plasma membrane engulfs food/materials from outside the cell. Amoeba acquires food this way.

flashcards.q: What is plasmolysis?
flashcards.a: Shrinkage and pulling away of the protoplasm/cell contents from the cell wall when a living plant cell loses water in a hypertonic solution.

flashcards.q: Why does plasmolysis NOT occur in dead (boiled) cells?
flashcards.a: Boiling kills cells and destroys the plasma membrane's selective permeability. Osmosis requires a living, selectively permeable membrane — dead cells cannot show osmosis or plasmolysis.

flashcards.q: Cell wall — composition, location, presence.
flashcards.a: Made of cellulose (in plants). Located OUTSIDE the plasma membrane. Present in: plants, fungi, bacteria. ABSENT in animal cells.

flashcards.q: Why can plant cells withstand hypotonic media without bursting?
flashcards.a: The rigid cell wall exerts an equal and opposite pressure (wall pressure) that resists swelling, preventing the cell from bursting even as it absorbs water by osmosis.

flashcards.q: What is the nucleoid? In which organisms?
flashcards.a: The undefined nuclear region in prokaryotes where DNA is located — no nuclear membrane surrounds it. Found in bacteria and other prokaryotes.

flashcards.q: Structure of the nucleus in eukaryotes?
flashcards.a: Double-layered nuclear membrane with pores (allow material transfer to cytoplasm). Inside: chromatin (DNA + protein). Chromatin condenses into chromosomes during cell division.

flashcards.q: What is chromatin? How does it differ from chromosomes?
flashcards.a: Chromatin: loose, thread-like DNA + protein in a non-dividing cell. Chromosomes: condensed, rod-shaped form of chromatin visible when the cell is about to divide.

flashcards.q: What are genes?
flashcards.a: Functional segments of DNA that carry information for constructing and organising cells and for inheritance of specific traits from parents to offspring.

flashcards.q: Prokaryote vs Eukaryote — 4 differences.
flashcards.a: Size: 1–10 µm vs 5–100 µm. Nuclear membrane: absent (nucleoid) vs present. Membrane-bound organelles: absent vs present. Chromosomes: single vs multiple.

flashcards.q: What is the cytoplasm?
flashcards.a: Fluid content inside the plasma membrane, excluding the nucleus. Contains all membrane-bound organelles in eukaryotic cells. Takes up very little stain.

flashcards.q: RER — structure and function?
flashcards.a: Rough ER: has RIBOSOMES on surface → protein synthesis. Proteins are then transported through ER to Golgi. RER contributes to membrane biogenesis (proteins for cell membrane).

flashcards.q: SER — structure and function?
flashcards.a: Smooth ER: NO ribosomes → synthesises LIPIDS important for cell function. In liver cells, SER detoxifies drugs and poisons. Contributes lipids for membrane biogenesis.

flashcards.q: Golgi apparatus — structure and functions?
flashcards.a: Stacked, membrane-bound flattened sacs (cisterns) connected to ER. Functions: (1) Packages and dispatches cell products, (2) Modifies proteins/lipids, (3) Makes complex sugars, (4) Forms lysosomes.

flashcards.q: What are lysosomes? Why called 'suicide bags'?
flashcards.a: Membrane-bound sacs filled with digestive enzymes (made by RER). Digest foreign material and old organelles. When they burst (cell damage), enzymes digest the cell — hence 'suicide bags'.

flashcards.q: Mitochondria — structure and function?
flashcards.a: Double membrane: porous outer + deeply folded inner (cristae — increase surface area). Function: produce ATP (energy currency) through cellular respiration. Have own DNA and ribosomes.

flashcards.q: What is ATP? Why called energy currency?
flashcards.a: ATP (Adenosine Triphosphate): produced by mitochondria. Used for: (1) making new chemical compounds, (2) mechanical work (muscle contraction, organelle movement), (3) active transport. 'Currency' because it is used and exchanged for all energy needs.

flashcards.q: Two organelles with their own DNA and ribosomes?
flashcards.a: Mitochondria and Plastids (chloroplasts). They can manufacture some of their own proteins and divide independently — semi-autonomous organelles.

flashcards.q: Types of plastids and their functions?
flashcards.a: Chromoplasts (coloured): Chloroplasts — contain chlorophyll, site of photosynthesis. Leucoplasts (colourless): store starch, oils, and protein granules. Present in plant cells ONLY.

flashcards.q: Vacuoles — compare in plant vs animal cells.
flashcards.a: Plant: large central vacuole (50–90% of cell volume), full of cell sap, provides turgidity and stores amino acids/sugars/organic acids/waste. Animal: small, multiple, scattered vacuoles. Amoeba: food vacuole.

flashcards.q: Plant cell vs Animal cell — 3 key differences.
flashcards.a: Plant: cell wall (cellulose), plastids, large central vacuole. Animal: no cell wall, no plastids, only small vacuoles.

flashcards.q: Where are lipids and proteins of the cell membrane made?
flashcards.a: Lipids: Smooth ER. Proteins: Ribosomes on Rough ER. Both travel to Golgi → packaged → fused with plasma membrane. Process = membrane biogenesis.

flashcards.q: Mitosis vs Meiosis — key differences.
flashcards.a: Mitosis: growth and repair → 1 mother cell → 2 daughter cells with SAME chromosome number. Meiosis: gamete formation → 2 consecutive divisions → 4 daughter cells with HALF chromosome number.

flashcards.q: Why does meiosis produce half the chromosome number?
flashcards.a: So that when two gametes (egg + sperm) fuse at fertilisation, the resulting zygote has the full chromosome number — preventing chromosome doubling each generation.

flashcards.q: Why are viruses not considered living outside a host cell?
flashcards.a: Viruses lack a plasma membrane and all organelles (no ribosomes for protein synthesis, no mitochondria for ATP). They cannot metabolise, grow, or reproduce independently. They hijack the host cell's organelles to function.
